finding_results.notes: Hermione's pity explanation (line 157) revised from thesis-formulation ('doing maths', 'let the first thing people know about me be the worst thing') to scene-bound speech grounded in the immediate moment ('They do what you're doing right now. Look at it, then look at me, and their whole face changes'). Added interruptions ('they—', 'and I can't—') and physical pressure marker (jaw setting). Draco's apology refusal (line 175) revised from generalized transaction-theory ('sorry turns it into a transaction. I say it, you answer it, we both pretend the account moved') to stumbled, physically grounded speech with the scar as anchor ('His thumb pressed into the L', 'He looked at the word under his hand', 'This doesn't get smaller'). Added multiple self-interruptions ('Not because I don't—', 'And then we've—', 'makes it—') and replaced narrator commentary ('The safer version of the sentence tried to take over and failed') with the dialogue's own broken texture. Both passages now tied to immediate scene through physical reference and include audible pressure markers per acceptance test.

summary: Revised two dialogue passages in the scar-reveal scene to replace composed thesis-language with pressured, scene-bound speech. Hermione's explanation of why she hides the scar now anchors to Draco's visible reaction in the room rather than delivering a generalized formulation about pity. Draco's refusal to apologize now stumbles through the idea physically grounded in the scar under his hand rather than articulating a clean theory of apology-as-transaction. Both passages gained interruptions, restarts, and body-reference while preserving their core dramatic content: her refusal to be reduced to pity, his refusal to offer a clearinghouse apology. No changes to surrounding dialogue or narration, which was already alive and pressure-true.
